responses: I live in Wright, Wyoming and have been in Wright and Wyoming for 20 years. I grew up in Wells, Minnesota
I have been an educator for 31 years with 20 being in the Campbell County School District.

As a legislator, I’ll tackle rural healthcare by backing smart, stable funding that keeps our local clinics and hospitals open without hitting property owners with higher taxes. We need to expand telehealth so folks in remote areas can connect right with specialists, and I’ll push for state matching funds to make that happen. I also want to grow our medical residency programs and offer student loan forgiveness to get doctors and nurses to put down roots in our rural counties.

When it comes to keeping our workforce strong and supporting our schools, I'll fight for predictable, inflation-adjusted funding so our districts can recruit and keep great teachers. I’ll also work to connect our K-12 schools, community colleges, and local businesses to build better technical and career training pipelines. This gives our young people a clear path to great Wyoming jobs right here at home.

To make healthcare more affordable, we have to cut through the red tape and look at practical solutions. First, we need to take a hard look at prescription drug costs. By forcing more transparency on pharmacy benefit managers, we can make sure discounts actually get passed down to folks at the pharmacy counter.

We should also give small businesses more flexibility to pool together and buy group insurance, which would drive down monthly premiums for working families. Finally, we need to cut the administrative paperwork cluttering up our rural clinics. When doctors spend less time filling out state forms, they can spend more time treating patients, which lowers overhead costs for everyone. By focusing on common-sense transparency, cutting waste, and boosting our local community health centers, we can keep high-quality care within reach without breaking the bank.

When it comes to our public lands, I’m 100% against selling or transferring them to private interests. These spaces are a massive part of Wyoming's heritage, tourism, and outdoor lifestyle. To make sure they're managed in the best interest of everyone, the Legislature needs to foster strong, common-sense partnerships with local ranchers, conservationists, energy producers, and federal agencies. We need a balanced, multi-use approach that fully supports our traditional industries like agriculture and energy, while firmly protecting open access for hunting, fishing, and outdoor recreation. By keeping local Wyoming voices at the head of the table, we can protect our beautiful landscapes and support our economy for generations to come.

Balancing property tax relief with local revenue needs is all about finding a middle ground. We need to protect homeowners without cutting the legs out from under the vital services our counties rely on, like our local law enforcement, emergency services, and roads.

To do that, we should expand the state's property tax refund program so it gives immediate, meaningful help to seniors, veterans, and working families who are really feeling the squeeze right now. We also need to put a sensible cap on how much annual assessments can jump so people aren't blindsided by massive tax spikes. To make sure our communities don't lose out, the state should use its budget surpluses to backfill that revenue for the counties. This keeps our local emergency services and roads fully funded while giving everyday Wyoming residents some much-needed breathing room.

The Legislature definitely has a big role to play in economic development, but it shouldn't be about the state picking winners and losers with your tax dollars. Instead, our job is to build a rock-solid foundation so local businesses can thrive and new industries actually want to set up shop here.

I truly feel that the system we have in place right now works well. From our end on the campaign trail, my team and I make it a point to input all of our donation and expense information weekly. Keeping up with it regularly like that makes sure the reporting doesn't turn into some massive, daunting task right before the deadlines, and it keeps us completely accountable to the public in real-time. Because our current rules do a great job of keeping things transparent and are straightforward enough for candidates to manage responsibly, I don't see a need for major reforms right now.

questions: Where do you live currently? How long have you lived there? How long have you lived in Wyoming? Where were you born?
What age will you be on Election Day, Nov. 3, 2026?
Please tell the voters about yourself including your background and qualifications.
What are the biggest challenges and opportunities facing Wyoming today? As a legislator, how would you address them?

We asked WyoFile readers to rank issues that are important to them, and healthcare costs and access topped the list. What can the Legislature do to make healthcare more affordable and accessible to Wyomingites?
Do you support efforts to sell or transfer public land in Wyoming? How do you plan to ensure that state and federal land is managed in the best interest of the people of Wyoming?
No two Wyoming counties are the same when it comes to how they generate revenue. As a legislator, how would you approach the public's desire for property tax relief with local revenue needs?
What role, if any, should the Wyoming Legislature play in economic development?
Explain your values when it comes to campaign donations. Does Wyoming's system for monitoring campaign donations and expenditures need reform?
pageProps.questionnaireIntro:
The answers below were solicited from candidates via a written questionnaire created by WyoFile reporters and editors in June 2026. Responses are presented exactly as submitted, without fact-checking, wordsmithing or editing for grammar, punctuation or spelling errors. 

Legislative candidates were invited to respond to the questionnaire several times by email and by phone. Out of fairness to the candidates who met the deadline, WyoFile will not add responses after the guide's publication.
pageProps.aboutProject:
WyoFile produced this guide with coding and web development by Thomas Musselman, editing by Tennessee Watson and production assistance by Calla Shosh. Contact Tennessee Watson with questions, corrections or suggestions at <tennessee@wyofile.com>.

WyoFile is a Wyoming 501(c)3 nonprofit, independent, member-supported, public-interest news service reporting on the people, places and policy of Wyoming. WyoFile's [donor base](https://wyofile.com/supporters/) is politically diverse, including some donors who are candidates in this year's election. We maintain separation between sources of revenue and editorial direction, decisions and oversight. Acceptance of financial support does not constitute implied or actual endorsement of donors or their products, services or opinions, and does not confer editorial influence, nor does donor support imply any endorsement of WyoFile's reporting, commentary or editorial position.

This material is available for republication by select media outlets. Contact Collaborations Editor Rebecca Huntington at <rebecca@wyofile.com> to learn how.
